coredata.dc:title: Saccharide-originated fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by in-liquid plasma with controlled orderliness of carbon core through precursor alteration for selective and rapid metal ion detection
coredata.dc:creator: Treepet, S.
Chokradjaroen, C.
Kim, K.
Saito, N.
Watthanaphanit, A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have grown interested in replacing metal-based quantum dots due to their fluorescent properties with low toxicity. The green synthesis, therefore, represents a contribution to this research field. Here, we demonstrate the synthesis of CDs from simple saccharides, including fructose, glucose, and sucrose, using an eco-friendly method—solution plasma (SP)—by applying plasma in the aqueous solution of the precursor. Variations and characterizations are conducted to understand the role or influence of each synthesis parameter, i.e. saccharide precursor's molecular structure, solution pH (2, 5, and 8), and post-treatment method (freeze-drying and heat-drying), in the materials' structure and fluorescent properties. Blue light-emitting CDs are prepared from all precursors of pH 5 and 8 through SP, followed by heat-drying. Results show that SP is responsible for the carbon core formation, and thus the precursor type affects the carbon core's orderliness. Heat-drying causes oxidation on the CDs' surface, whereby OH functional groups change to C=O. Among all samples, the CDs synthesized from fructose at pH 5 (Fru-CDs-5) perform the best photoluminescence property, having the highest quantum yield (QY) of 9.16% and exhibiting an outstanding ability to detect Fe3+ ions. This is the first attempt to evince the potential use of SP to synthesize CDs from natural carbon sources and obtain CDs exhibiting superior fluorescence properties without any doping and complex synthesis steps. In addition to offering a new synthetic approach, this work specifies the role of the SP and the effect of the saccharide's molecular structure, which is the most relevant synthesis parameters mandatory to obtain tailored CDs with precise control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 0D carbon nanomaterial
Simple saccharide
Solution plasma
Carbon core formation
Detection of metal ion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of metal ions
Effect of the precursor type, post-treatment method, and solution pH
Selective and sensitive detection of metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: School of Aerospace Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The first author is funded by the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) for her Ph.D. study. This funder also gives support to chemicals and materials. The authors would also like to express their appreciation to Mahidol University Frontier Research Facility (MU-FRF) for supporting equipment for characterizations. Thanks are also to Dr. Atitaya Siripinyanond, an Associate Professor at the Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, who provides all metal ions for fluorescence quenching testing and much valuable advice in the early stages of this work.

The first author is funded by the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand ( SAST ) for her Ph.D. study. This funder also gives support to chemicals and materials. The authors would also like to express their appreciation to Mahidol University Frontier Research Facility (MU-FRF) for supporting equipment for characterizations. Thanks are also to Dr. Atitaya Siripinyanond, an Associate Professor at the Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, who provides all metal ions for fluorescence quenching testing and much valuable advice in the early stages of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram depicting the experimental setup and overall synthesis of CDs from fructose, glucose, and sucrose using SP. CD, carbon dot; SP, solution plasma.

Photographs of the CD solutions prepared from fructose (Fru-CDs), glucose (Glu-CDs), and sucrose (Suc-CDs) of different pHs and post-treatment methods: (a) freeze-drying and (b) heat-drying, under regular and UV 365 nm light. CD, carbon dot; UV, ultraviolet.

Schematic illustration of possible reaction pathways for CD formation through SP from fructose and glucose. Starting from (a) isomerization between glucose and fructose occurring under acidic and alkaline conditions through the 1,2-enediol intermediate formation. Possible reaction pathways and the related products in (b) acidic media (pH 2) and (c) pH 5 and 8, along with the post-treatment aid by heat-drying. CD, carbon dot; SP, solution plasma.

(a) FTIR spectra of the CDs prepared from fructose of pH 5 (Fru-CDs-5) post-treated by freeze-drying and heat-drying. (b) The carbonyl to hydroxyl ratios (C=O/O–H) of the freeze-dried and heat-dried CDs synthesized from different precursors at pH 5 and 8. CD, carbon dot; FTIR, Fourier transform infrared.

(a) UV–vis spectra of Fru-CDs, Glu-CDs, and Suc-CDs synthesized at pH 5 and 8, post-treated by heat-drying. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Fru-CDs-5 at different excitation wavelengths. The inset photographs show the as-prepared CDs under regular (left) and UV 365 nm light (right). (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of each CD at its maximum excitation wavelength, i.e. Ex. 400 nm for all CDs, except Glu-CDs-8 (Ex. 380 nm). (d) The CDs' QY. CD, carbon dot; QY, quantum yield; UV, ultraviolet.

(a) Raman spectra of Fru-CDs, Glu-CDs, and Suc-CDs synthesized at pH 5 and 8, post-treated by heat-drying, along with the corresponding ID/IG ratios. (b) Relation among the ID/IG ratios, bandgaps, and C=O/O–H ratios of the correspondence CDs. (c) Hydrodynamic diameters. (d) Selected TEM images of Fru-CDs and Glu-CDs synthesized at pH 5 and 8. (e) High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of a single particle of each CD synthesized at pH 5. CD, carbon dot; TEM, transmission electron microscope.

UV–vis spectra of Fru-CDs-5 solutions containing; (a) Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+, along with photographs of the solutions under UV 365 nm light, and (b) Fe3+, as a function of Fe3+ concentration. (c) A graph of Fru-CDs-5 shows linear relationship between A/A0 against Fe3+ concentration. (d) The interference test results. CD, carbon dot; UV, ultraviolet.

Schematic representation of CD formation through SP depending on the precursor type, combined with the essential post-treatment step by heat-drying for surface oxidation and thus the CDs' photoluminescence property relating to the QY and energy bandgap of pH 5 samples. CD, carbon dot; SP, solution plasma; QY, quantum yield.
The LOD of Fe3+ ions detectable by all CDs employing UV–vis spectroscopy and naked eyes under UV 365 nm light.

head.ce:title: Saccharide-originated fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by in-liquid plasma with controlled orderliness of carbon core through precursor alteration for selective and rapid metal ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, Salaya, Nakhon Pathom, 73170, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, Salaya, Nakhon Pathom, 73170, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Systems Engineering, Graduate School of Engineering, Nagoya University, Furo-cho, Chikusa-ku, Nagoya, 464-8603, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Systems Engineering, Graduate School of Engineering, Nagoya University, Furo-cho, Chikusa-ku, Nagoya, 464-8603, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have grown interested in replacing metal-based quantum dots due to their fluorescent properties with low toxicity. The green synthesis, therefore, represents a contribution to this research field. Here, we demonstrate the synthesis of CDs from simple saccharides, including fructose, glucose, and sucrose, using an eco-friendly method—solution plasma (SP)—by applying plasma in the aqueous solution of the precursor. Variations and characterizations are conducted to understand the role or influence of each synthesis parameter, i.e. saccharide precursor's molecular structure, solution pH (2, 5, and 8), and post-treatment method (freeze-drying and heat-drying), in the materials' structure and fluorescent properties. Blue light-emitting CDs are prepared from all precursors of pH 5 and 8 through SP, followed by heat-drying. Results show that SP is responsible for the carbon core formation, and thus the precursor type affects the carbon core's orderliness. Heat-drying causes oxidation on the CDs' surface, whereby OH functional groups change to C=O. Among all samples, the CDs synthesized from fructose at pH 5 (Fru-CDs-5) perform the best photoluminescence property, having the highest quantum yield (QY) of 9.16% and exhibiting an outstanding ability to detect Fe3+ ions. This is the first attempt to evince the potential use of SP to synthesize CDs from natural carbon sources and obtain CDs exhibiting superior fluorescence properties without any doping and complex synthesis steps. In addition to offering a new synthetic approach, this work specifies the role of the SP and the effect of the saccharide's molecular structure, which is the most relevant synthesis parameters mandatory to obtain tailored CDs with precise control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanomaterial consisting of aromatic/aliphatic carbon core (i.e. sp2 hybridization of graphene island and sp3 hybridization of diamond inclusion) and desirous surface functional groups (e.g. hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine). They are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with a dimension size below 10 nm [1]. Over the past decade, research studies on CDs have proliferated regarding their fluorescent properties originating from the quantum confinement effect of conjugated π-domains and defect state emission. The former relates to the ordering of carbon core and size [2], while the latter links to their surface functional group and surface passivation [3–5]. CDs can be synthesized by either a ‘top-down method’ like arc-discharge [6], electrochemical exfoliation [7], laser-ablation [8], and chemical oxidation [9], or a ‘bottom-up method’ like pyrolysis [10], hydrothermal [11], microwave [12], and ultrasonic [13]. The top-down method requires an extreme chemical oxidizing and/or high power applied energy to break down bulk carbon precursors such as carbon nanotubes, graphite oxide, soot, and coal into small nanoparticles [14]. On the contrary, the bottom-up method can be facile synthesis from several kinds of carbon precursors in a one-step procedure with simple equipment. The CDs with a well-defined monodispersed structure and desired functional groups can also be obtained. These are reasons why the bottom-up approach is preferable.

The in-liquid plasma method, solution plasma (SP), is an alternative bottom-up method that can synthesize nanomaterials under mild conditions. During the SP, plasma is generated between nearby electrodes and induces the formation of reactive species to activate the reaction. For nanocarbon synthesis, the reactions resulting from the activation of reactive species are carbonization and/or polymerization depending on starting materials [15]. Recently, Kim et al. reported the successful synthesis of CDs through SP for the first time using a mixing solution of pyridine (C5H5N) and water as a precursor [16]. This report creates a facile one-step synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs with short-time consumption (within 10 min). It provides a high quantum yield (QY) value of 61% and excellent photoluminescence properties.

Saccharide-based CDs are choices for green synthesis and sustainable carbon precursors. The precursors in this group reported previously include glucose [17,18], galactose [19], lactose [20], fructose [18], maltose [21], sucrose [22], xylitol [21], cellulose [23], and alginate [24]. They have been utilized in drug delivery [18,25], photocatalytic [26], and sensing [27,28] applications. Most of these studies attempted to synthesize CDs using several approaches with condition modification to seek a more straightforward synthesis route that requires milder conditions and a smaller number of reagents but still provides CDs with comparable or enhanced QY values. Nevertheless, the study explaining the effect of saccharides' molecular structure on the CDs' formation mechanism is limited. Besides, since heteroatom doping is an efficient route to improve the QY, most studies use more than one starting material. For instance, Gude et al. synthesized CDs exhibiting orange-red emission from sucrose in the aqueous solution of phosphoric acid (H3PO4) via heating at 70 °C followed by column purification. H3PO4 acts as a dehydrating agent and produces H+ ions to function as a catalyst. This reaction is thus considered acid-catalyzed dehydration. They described the mechanism of CD formation through the emergence and aggregation of hydroxymethylfuran in an acidic environment [22]. Phosphorous in the system may additionally lead to P-doping CDs, a possible cause of photoluminescence properties. The synthesis of CDs by combining galactose and cysteine (C3H7NO2S) as precursors using a one-step hydrothermal refluxing process was reported by Wang et al. [29]. The reaction was performed in sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution, which was crucial for the formation of CDs with strong photoluminescence properties. It was supposed that CD formation occurred through Maillard reaction, generation of furan-like compounds, condensation and polymerization of furan-like molecules, carbonization and aromatization followed by many single nuclear bursts of aromatic clusters, and finally transformation into CDs. The presence of nitrogen in the system may lead to N-doping CDs and thus promote the samples' photoluminescence. Study reported the synthesis of saccharide-originated CDs without any passivating or doping additives has also been accessible. Cailotto et al. synthesized CDs from fructose, glucose, and ascorbic acid by hydrothermal at 200 °C for 24 h. They demonstrated how the precursor type influences the properties of the obtained CDs. However, the focus has been paid to the obtained CDs' drug-loading capabilities and their toxicity toward cancer cells [18]. Reports directly examining the effect of the saccharide precursors' molecular structure on the CD formation are almost non-existent. It should be postulated that although the synthesis of CDs from saccharide-based precursors seem to be common, preparation of the undoped-CDs with satisfactory QY values and the discussion on influence of the precursor's molecular structure under the method of interest have never been announced. These are the most relevant synthesis parameters mandatory to obtain tailored CDs with precise control.

Herein, we synthesized CDs from three saccharide precursors, including fructose, glucose, and sucrose, through the SP, marking the beginning of CD synthesis by SP from natural carbon sources. We chose these simple sugars because they are monosaccharide and disaccharide consisting of the 5-membered ring (fructose), 6-membered ring (glucose), and the combination of them (sucrose). Understanding the influence of molecular structure and size of the starting material on CD formation through the SP was the first aim of this study. The effect of solution pH, i.e. pH 2 (acidic), pH 5 (neutral), and pH 8 (basic), was investigated since this variable can affect the kind and intensity of reactive species in the SP system [30] and thus, the reaction routes for the CDs' carbon core formation [31]. Note that pH 5 was classified as neutral because this pH was achieved after the dissolution of saccharide precursors in water without pH adjustment. Furthermore, drying methods with different temperatures, so-called ‘post-treatment’ that can affect surface oxidation [32], including freeze-drying and heat-drying, were carried out. The CDs' surface chemistry, carbon core structure, morphology, hydrodynamic size, and photoluminescence property were investigated. Over and above that, CDs can generally be utilized as metal sensing with a high selectivity through luminescence quenching due to their specific surface functional groups. Utilizing CDs-based sensing can solve the problem of the standard methods for the quantitative determination of metal ions, i.e. atomic absorption spectrometry and inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry, in terms of expensive instruments, long-time processing, and specialists' requirement. Consequently, the applicability of the obtained CDs for selective and sensitive metal ions detection was eventually evaluated.

The present findings represent a straightforward, green approach to synthesizing CDs from simple saccharides. Utilizing the SP allows mild conditions, using only water as a solvent at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. The operation time could also be reduced compared to the popular hydrothermal-assisted method. The obtained products effectively determine the existence of Fe3+ ions precisely with a low limit of detection (LOD) and exhibit the benefits of rapid response, physiological pH, and room temperature experimental conditions. This is the first attempt to evince the potential use of SP to synthesize CDs from natural carbon sources and discuss the influence of the precursor's molecular structure and the prominent role of the synthesis method on CD formation.

Fructose (C6H12O6, 99.0% purity), glucose (C6H12O6, 98.0% purity), and sucrose (C12H22O11, 98.0% purity) were purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Co., Inc. (Tokyo, Japan). Quinine sulfate dihydrate (C40H52N4O9S, ≥99% purity) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98% purity) were supplied from Fluka (Germany). Sodium hydroxide pellet (NaOH, 99% purity) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) were purchased from ANaPURE® (Newzealand). CaCl2·2H2O (Riedel-deHaen), CuCl2·3H2O (Merck), Pb(NO3)2 (AccuTrade), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (AnalaR), Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (Fluka), and FeCl3 (Riedel-deHaen) were used as precursors of aqueous solution of the respective ions: Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+. All chemical reagents and solvents were of analytical grades and used without further purification. Type II water obtained from Gesellschaft Fuer Labortec™ (GFL 2012) water distillation apparatus was used throughout the experiments.

The Fru-CDs, Glu-CDs, and Suc-CDs were synthesized from 20 mM aqueous solutions of fructose, glucose, and sucrose, respectively, using the SP. A schematic diagram depicting the experimental setup and overall synthesis of CDs by SP is illustrated in Fig. 1 . A precursor solution of 100 mL was poured into the glass reactor equipped with a 1-mm diameter of tungsten rod electrodes. Then, plasma was generated by a bipolar pulse power supply (Kurita Seisakusho Co., Ltd.) with a pulse repetition frequency and pulse duration of 20 kHz and 0.9 μs, respectively. The plasma treatment time was fixed at 20 min. After that, the post-treatment process was conducted to dry and induce surface oxidation of the samples. Two post-treatment methods, including (i) freeze-drying (−80 °C, 48 h) and (ii) heat-drying (80 °C, 10 h), were used and studied in comparison, using a freeze dryer (FreezeZone Plus, Stoppering Tray Dryer) and an oven (Memmert, Universal Oven UF110). Apart from the effect of the post-treatment method, the impact of solution pH was studied. The pH of the precursor solution was adjusted to three pH ranges, i.e. pH 2, 5, and 8. The precursor solution in purified water having pH 5 was changed to acidic (pH 2) and basic (pH 8) using aqueous solutions of 1.0 mM HCl and 1.0 mM NaOH, respectively. Note that HCl and NaOH were used to adjust pH instead of buffer solutions effective in these pH ranges (i.e. phosphate, H3PO4; glycine, C2H5NO2; or tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, C4H11NO3) to remove the possible variables affecting the CD properties (i.e. doping element) other than the primary variables of this study (i.e. sugar type).

Before further characterization, the as-prepared CDs powder was redispersed in purified water to gain CD solutions of 10 mg/mL, filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter (Whatman®), and stored at 4 °C.

Functional groups on the CDs' surface were analyzed using an attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscope (Thermo Nicolet 6700). The CDs powder was dried at 40 °C overnight to remove moisture before testing. The spectra were collected from 4000 to 600 cm−1 in transmission mode with an average of 64 scans. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. Apart from finding the peak position, the results were converted through the calculation to determine the samples' QY and bandgap. The latter, i.e. energy bandgap (E g), was evaluated using the Tauc Plot method for direct bandgap, determined by the following equation [33]:
α h ν
= A
(
h ν −
E g
)
1 /n
where α, h, ν, and A are optical absorption coefficient, Plank constant, light frequency, and the proportionality constant, respectively. The natural electron transition is represented with n, where the value for direct-allowed transition is n = 1/2. Thus, the E g was found in the extrapolation of plots between (αhν)2 and hν by linear fitting.

The photoluminescence measurement was performed using a FluoroMax® 4 spectrofluorometer from Horiba Scientific, with a slit width of 3 nm for both excitation and emission. The QY of CDs was measured using the comparative method by comparing with quinine sulfate (QS) as a reference, determined by the following equation [34]:
Φ CDs
=
Φ CDs
(
I CDs
I Ref
A ref
A CDs
η CDs 2
η Ref 2
)
where ‘Φ’ is the QY, ‘I’ is the integrated fluorescence emission area, ‘A’ is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and ‘ƞ’ is the refractive index of the used solvent. The subscript ‘CDs’ refers to the CDs and ‘Ref’ to the reference QS solution with known QY (54%).

The carbon's molecular morphology was characterized using a Raman spectroscope (XploRA PLUS, Horiba Scientific) using CDs powder. The spectra were recorded with a 532-nm laser using 1200-g/mm grating and 10% filter. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi, HT-7700) investigated the CDs' morphology and size at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. The hydrodynamic size was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
First, all CD solutions exhibiting light emission under UV light, including Fru-CDs-5, Fru-CDs-8, Glu-CDs-5, Glu-CDs-8, Suc-CDs-5, and Suc-CDs-8, were tested. Note that the number written after the sample name indicates the pH value. Solutions of metal ions (stock concentration of 1 mM) with diverse species, including Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+, were prepared. Then 10 μL of the metal ion solution was injected into 1 mL of the CD solution (10 mg/mL). The mixture was shaken for 30 s and incubated for 60 s before observing fluorescence quenching under UV 365 nm light by the naked eyes. In addition, UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded, and the intensity of spectra at a wavelength of 280 nm was plotted against metal ion type. We found that only Fe3+ ions induced fluorescence quenching. The LOD of the CDs toward Fe3+ ions was further investigated by varying the concentrations of Fe3+ in purified water to 0.05 mM, 0.25 mM, 0.50 mM, 0.75 mM, and 1.00 mM and plotting the value of A/A0 against concentration when ‘A’ represents the absorbance of CDs in the presence of metal ions and ‘A0’ represents the absorbance of CDs without any metal ions. The LOD was calculated using the following equation [35]:
LOD=3.3×(SD of intercept/Slope)

Where ‘SD of intercept’ is the standard deviation of the y-intercept of the calibration curve, which can be calculated from the SE (standard error) of intercept obtained from linear regression. Specifically, SD of intercept = SE of intercept × √N, when ‘N’ is the number of tests (here, N = 5). ‘Slope’ is the slope of the calibration curve.
Finally, an interference test was conducted using Fru-CDs-5 as representing CDs by adding another metal ion in the condition containing Fe3+ and observing UV–vis absorbance change.

This study is interested in the role of SP, and the factors affecting the formation of CDs synthesized through SP. Fig. 2 shows photographs of the CD solutions prepared from fructose, glucose, and sucrose of different pHs and post-treatment methods under regular and UV 365 nm light. We first considered the analysis under the effect of three factors: (i) the precursor type, (ii) the post-treatment method, and (iii) the pH of the precursor solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the precursor type, post-treatment method, and solution pH
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 demonstrates that while only freeze-dried Fru-CDs and Suc-CDs at pH 2 changed to light brown, the solution color of all heat-dried CDs changed to brown. Besides, the color change was significant at pH 2 in Fru-CDs and Suc-CDs, in which a 5-membered ring exists in the precursor molecule. The conversion of fructose and glucose in acidic media at elevated temperature has been reported by van Dam et al. [36], with a possible reaction pathway illustrated in Fig. 3 a. Then, fructose is dehydrated to form hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) via 1,2-enediol intermediate (reaction (i) in Fig. 3b). Apart from HMF, other dehydration products, including levulinic acid, formic acid, and a carbon-based macromolecular substance called ‘humine’—a leading cause of brown matter formation—have been found. Glucose conversion to HMF and the dehydrated products via this reaction was also possible but with a much lower reactivity than fructose [36]. For this reason, the formation of the brown matter was noticeable in 5-membered ring-containing molecules, i.e. fructose and sucrose, at low pH.

Fig. 2 additionally points out that the SP could prepare color-emitting CDs with post-treatment aid by heat-drying. In other words, the post-treatment with heat was crucial for obtaining the CDs with color emission under UV light. Consequently, attention will be paid to the heat-dried samples. We hypothesized that this phenomenon is caused by surface oxidation after heating. FTIR spectroscopy analysis was conducted to evaluate the surface functional group to prove this assumption, shown in the next section.

Considering the heat-dried samples regarding the effect of pH, the results demonstrated light emission under UV light (blue emission) at pH 5 and 8, but not pH 2. As mentioned above, the finding at pH 2 could be due to brown matter formation in acidic media that may hinder the CDs' light emission. On the other hand, the results suggested no macromolecular carbon formation at pH 5 and 8. It has been reported that isomerization between glucose and fructose could also occur under alkaline conditions through the 1,2-enediol intermediate formation (Fig. 3a) [37]. The results obtained led us to propose a reaction pathway, as shown in Fig. 3a–c, that the SP may induce ring-opening and promote CDs' formation through the arrangement of enediol intermediate, followed by polymerization (reaction (ii) in Fig. 3c). A more detailed interpretation is presented in the last section. Since the samples synthesized at pH 5 and 8, post-treated by heat-drying, give rise to the formation of light-emitting CDs, attention will be paid to these samples for further discussion and characterization.

One more essential factor controlling the physicochemical properties of CDs is derived primarily from surface functional groups. We, therefore, used FTIR to confirm the change in surface functional groups on the heat-dried samples. Selected attenuated total reflectance-FTIR spectra of the CDs, i.e. Fru-CDs-5, post-treated by freeze-drying, and heat-drying, are shown in Fig. 4 a. Both freeze-dried and heat-dried CDs exhibited absorption bands at 3600–3200 cm−1 (O–H stretching), 3200–3000 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1720–1660 cm−1 (C=O stretching), 1620–1420- cm−1 (C=C bending), and 1150–1020 cm−1 (C–O stretching), suggesting the functional groups existing on the CDs' surface. Comparing the freeze-dried and the heat-dried CDs, the peak intensity of the O–H functional group decreased while the carbonyl group (C=O) peak was more remarkable in the heat-dried sample. The spectra of all samples are shown in Fig. S1, where each sample's ratio between the intensity of carbonyl to hydroxyl (C=O/O–H) peaks was evaluated and shown in Fig. 4b. Results verified that, except for the Suc-CDs-8, the C=O/O–H ratios of all heat-dried CDs were higher than the freeze-dried CDs, suggesting a greater degree of oxidation (conversion of O–H to C=O) at higher temperature upon heating [38]. Furthermore, the degree of C=O/O–H ratios of CDs synthesized from different precursors was in the following order: Fru-CDs > Glu-CDs > Suc-CDs, agreeing with the intensity of noticeable emitted light under UV light as shown in Fig. 2b. This result suggested that the increase of C=O on the CDs' surface correlates with the improved samples' photoluminescence properties, further confirmed by QY evaluation.

The quantity of light emission of CDs after the absorption of photons, also known as photoluminescence, was determined by QY evaluation from UV–vis and fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 5 . The UV–vis spectra of all CDs (Fig. 5a) exhibited two electronic transitions at 240 nm and 280 nm relating to π-π∗ transition of C=C bonds from conjugated carbon and n-π∗ transition of C=O and C–O bonds, respectively. Considering the effect of pH, the n-π∗ transition of Suc-CDs-8 (pH 8) remarkably shifted from 280 to 301 nm (bathochromic shift) due to the abundant hydroxyl groups. Regarding the type of precursor, the peak of n-π∗ transition at 343 nm was noticeable in Fru-CDs and Glu-CDs, but not Suc-CDs, in both pHs. This peak is defined as the trapping of excited-state energy by a surface state, enhancing fluorescence intensity [39]. The observation corresponded to the light seen with eyes under UV light.

For fluorescence measurement, the sweep range of fluorescence emission from 300 to 700 nm was scanned to find the excitation wavelength that exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity, using different wavelengths for excitation ranging from 280 to 500 nm with 20 nm intervals. The results of a selected sample, i.e. Fru-CDs-5, are shown in Fig. 5b, along with the results of all samples shown in Fig. S2. Except for Glu-CDs-8, the maximum fluorescence intensity of all CDs was found at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Furthermore, while spectra at low excitation wavelengths (280–400 nm) increased progressively and showed an excitation-independent behavior, the fluorescence behavior changed to excitation-dependent after 400 nm. Also, fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with increasing wavelength. For Glu-CDs-8 (see Fig. S2), fluorescence intensity was maximum, and light emission behavior changed from excitation-independent to excitation-dependent at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Emission wavelengths at maxima fluorescence intensity of peaks of Fru-CDs-5 at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S3.

Fluorescence emission spectra of each CD at its maximum excitation wavelength are lined up in Fig. 5c. Consistent with the results mentioned above, the type of precursor influenced the optical properties of the obtained CDs by showing fluorescence intensity in the following order: Fru-CDs > Glu-CDs > Suc-CDs. Besides, pH increment led to lowering fluorescence intensity. The QY values quantitatively representing photoluminescence properties of all CDs are exhibited in Fig. 5d. The most excellent QY was found in Fru-CDs-5, with a value of 9.16%. Although the value is relatively low, it is pretty good without heteroatom doping. Photoluminescence properties of saccharide-based CDs synthesized by several means, as reported previously, are shown in Table S1 for comparison.

The formation of CDs was further characterized using Raman spectroscopy to determine the well-ordered and disordered carbon core structures by evaluating the quantitative index of intensity D to G (ID/IG) ratio. Fig. 6 a shows Raman spectra of Fru-CDs, Glu-CDs, and Suc-CDs synthesized at pH 5 and 8, presenting D band (1335 cm−1) and G band (1570 cm−1), indicating defect and in-plane symmetry of sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively. The ID/IG ratios referring to the disordered degree of the CDs' carbon core are additionally shown. These values are compared and illustrated in Fig. 6b. Considering the type of precursor, ID/IG values of the obtained CDs were in the following order: Fru-CDs < Glu-CDs ≈ Suc-CDs. Significantly lower ID/IG value of Fru-CDs (ID/IG = 0.80) than the others indicated orderliness of in-plane sp2 carbon atoms in the core of Fru-CDs. The bandgap of all CDs was calculated and shown, along with the C=O/O–H ratios for consideration in Fig. 6b. It was found that the less disorder of the CDs' core (low ID/IG), the lower the energy gap between LUMO and HOMO. Moreover, C=O/O–H ratios increased with the reduced energy gap [40]. These findings suggested that Fru-CDs provided the highest photoluminescence property because of two realizable reasons: (i) the more organized carbon core structure and (ii) the greater amount of C=O functional groups on the CDs' surface relating to surface oxidation.

Fig. 6c shows the CDs' hydrodynamic size (H d). The observed most petite H d of Fru-CDs could also recommend their well-ordered carbon core structure. Furthermore, the size of the resulting CDs was directly proportional to the precursor's molecular size, i.e. Fru-CDs < Glu-CDs < Suc-CDs. The solution pH was likely to affect the H d. More alkaline conditions gave rise to larger H d due to the enormous amount of hydroxide ions which can induce hydrogen bonding between CDs' surface and water [31]. TEM images confirming zero-dimensional (0D) features with diameters of less than 10 nm of Fru-CDs and Glu-CDs synthesized at pH 5 and 8 are illustrated in Fig. 6d. Considering the particle size synthesized at pH 5, the average diameters of Fru-CDs and Glu-CDs were 1.8 and 5.6 nm; while that of Suc-CDs was significantly larger, i.e. 101.9 nm. Diameter size distributions are shown in Fig. S4. The high-resolution TEM images of a single particle of each CD at pH 5 are additionally shown in Fig. 6e. Since Fru-CDs were very tiny, we cannot notice the lattice fringe from the image. However, it is notable that the crystalline nature of Glu-CDs and Suc-CDs was monocrystalline and semicrystalline, with a lattice space of approximately 0.17 and 0.22, respectively. These results confirm the sequence of carbon core orderliness as follows: Fru-CDs > Glu-CDs > Suc-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Photographs of all CD solutions containing Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+ under UV 365 nm light are shown in Fig. S5. Results of a selected sample, i.e. Fru-CDs-5, are shown in Fig. 7 a. Only the quenching effect with Fe3+ ions was strong enough to observe by naked eyes. This paves the way for selective Fe3+ ions detection. We furthermore found that UV–vis spectroscopy could identify the quenching effect in this system. As shown in Fig. 7a, all samples had their most substantial absorption absorbance at a wavelength of 280 nm (λ max = 280 nm). Besides, the intensity at λ max of CDs containing Fe3+ ions was significantly greater than the CDs containing other metal ions.

The effect of Fe3+ ion concentration on the absorbance of CD solution was further explored by testing five different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorbance increased with the increasing Fe3+ concentration, confirming the dependent absorbance on Fe3+ ion concentration. The LOD over a concentration of 0–100 μM was consequently evaluated through UV–vis absorption by finding a linear relationship between A/A0 against Fe3+ concentration. The resulting graphs, along with related linear regression equations and R2 values of all CDs, are shown in Fig. S6. Among these, Fru-CDs-5 exhibited the most outstanding efficiency, offering the lowest LOD of 0.0065 mM with R2 of 0.9997 using UV–vis spectroscopy and 0.25 mM with naked eyes through fluorescence quenching under UV light (see Fig. 7c). Suvarnaphaet et al. demonstrated that photoluminescence quenching of the CDs with Fe3+ ions occurred via electron transfer from oxygen-containing groups of the CDs surface to Fe3+ ions [27]. The prominence property of Fru-CDs-5 over the others could likely, therefore, result from a co-factor between the higher C=O functional groups being a more potent electron donor regarding their conjugation and the lower energy bandgap of the sample for easier fluorescence quenching. The LOD of Fe3+ ions detectable by all CDs employing UV–vis spectroscopy and naked eyes under UV light are listed in Table 1 .

Finally, an interference test was conducted using Fru-CDs-5 as representing CDs by adding another metal ion in the condition containing Fe3+ ions and observing UV–vis absorbance change. The results (Fig. 7d) indicated that Fru-CDs-5 was specific to Fe3+, although other metal ions additionally existed in the system.

Consolidating the experimental findings above, the role of SP for CD synthesis is the construction of the carbon core. As reported by Morishita et al. the formation of nanocarbons in the SP system depends on the type of starting solution [15]. For saturated ring molecules (e.g. cyclohexane), the same type of molecule as fructose, glucose, and sucrose; nanocarbon formation starts from (i) C–H dissociation, followed by (ii) conversion into unsaturated ring molecules, and (iii) polymerization through cation radicals. The first step of this reaction (i.e. C–H dissociation) agrees with the ring-opening of fructose and glucose shown above in Fig. 3a. Fru-CDs' observed a greater photoluminescence property over Glu-CDs and Suc-CDs could reasonably be attributed to the respective better carbon core's orderliness (Fru-CDs > Glu-CDs > Suc-CDs). We divided the explanation of these results into two main issues: (I) the effect of monosaccharides vs. disaccharides and (II) the effect of 5- vs. 6-membered rings for monosaccharides.

For issue (I), we speculated that the arrangement of carbon cores of Fru-CDs and Glu-CDs during SP could be more organized than Suc-CDs because their precursors are monosaccharides. In contrast, the Suc-CDs' precursor is a disaccharide containing both 5- and 6-membered rings. For issue (II), the reason contributing to a better carbon core arrangement of Fru-CDs over Glu-CDs could be the more extraordinary ability of the 5-membered ring molecules to form 1,2-enediol intermediate and thus carbon core formation. This assumption relates to the lower dissociation enthalpies of C–H bonds in the 5-membered rings than the 6-membered ring due to the higher strain destabilization in the molecule [41].

Fig. 8 shows a schematic representation of CD formation through SP depending on the precursor type. Even so, the post-treatment aid by heat-drying to generate functional groups on the CDs' surface is crucial for the CDs' photoluminescence property. The QY and energy bandgap values of pH 5 samples are additionally shown.

CDs with blue light emission under UV light were successfully synthesized from fructose, glucose, and sucrose by applying plasma in their aqueous solutions, then post-treatment by heat-drying. The SP was responsible for the carbon core formation occurring through the arrangement and polymerization of precursor molecules, while the heat-drying was in charge of surface oxidation, whereby the OH functional group on CDs' surface changed to C=O. Two realizable reasons contributing to CDs with good photoluminescence properties are (i) the more organized carbon core structure and (ii) the greater amount of C=O functional groups on the CDs' surface. Consequently, the post-treatment by heat-drying was crucial, and the QY values of the obtained CDs were in the following order: Fru-CDs > Glu-CDs > Suc-CDs. The latter could be attributed to the more simple structural unit of fructose and glucose over sucrose. Specifically, the arrangement of carbon cores of Fru-CDs and Glu-CDs during SP could be more organized than Suc-CDs because their precursors are monosaccharides. In contrast, the Suc-CDs' precursor is a disaccharide containing both 5- and 6-membered rings. The carbon core's orderliness additionally affected the CDs' H d and energy bandgap. The greater orderliness of the carbon core, the more petite H d and lower bandgap. The solution pH also influenced this synthesis. The maximum QY was found in Fru-CDs-5, demonstrating the QY value of 9.16%. Furthermore, it displayed outstanding sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ ions with a detection limit of 0.0065 mM using UV–vis spectroscopy and 0.25 mM by naked eye through fluorescence quenching under UV light. This research demonstrates a facile and mild synthesis of CDs from natural substances without adding acid, base, passivating, and doping additives, producing an alternative means with rapid CD formation. We expect that understanding the influence of molecular structure and size of saccharide precursor for CD synthesis through SP will help select appropriate starting materials for the synthesis with this approach in other systems.

Sasimaporn Treepet: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. Chayanaphat Chokradjaroen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Kyusung Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Nagahiro Saito: Conceptualization, Resources, Validation. Anyarat Watthanaphanit: Conceptualization, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The first author is funded by the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand (SAST) for her Ph.D. study. This funder also gives support to chemicals and materials. The authors would also like to express their appreciation to Mahidol University Frontier Research Facility (MU-FRF) for supporting equipment for characterizations. Thanks are also to Dr. Atitaya Siripinyanond, an Associate Professor at the Faculty of Science, Mahidol University, who provides all metal ions for fluorescence quenching testing and much valuable advice in the early stages of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article: FTIR spectra of (a) Fru-CDs-5, (b) Fru-CDs-8, (c) Glu-CDs-5, (d) Glu-CDs-8, (e) Suc-CDs-5, and (f) Suc-CDs-8, post-treated by freeze-drying and heat-drying; Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Fru-CDs-5, (b) Fru-CDs-8, (c) Glu-CDs-5, (d) Glu-CDs-8, (e) Suc-CDs-5, and (f) Suc-CDs-8, at different excitation wavelengths; Emission wavelengths at maxima fluorescence intensity of peaks of Fru-CDs-5 at different excitation wavelengths; Diameter size distributions of Fru-CDs, Glu-CDs, and Suc-CDs synthesized at pH 5; Photoluminescence properties of saccharide-based CDs synthesized by several means, as reported previously; Photographs of all CDs solutions containing Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+ under UV 365 nm light; The linear relationship between A/A0 against Fe3+ concentration of (a) Fru-CDs-5, (b) Fru-CDs-8, (c) Glu-CDs-5, (d) Glu-CDs-8, (e) Suc-CDs-5, and (f) Suc-CDs-8, along with related linear regression equations and R2 values.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2022.101139.

Zhang, Phosphorus, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for real-time measurement of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species inside macrophages, Biosens. Bioelectron. 79 (2016) 822-828, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2016.01.022.
